content.problemStatement:
You have NNN cards placed in front of you on the table. The ithi^{th}ith card has the number AiA_iAi​ written on it.

In one move, you can **remove** any one card from the remaining cards on the table.

Find the **minimum** number of moves required so that all the cards remaining on the table have the **same** number written on them.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the number of cards on the table.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ where AiA_iAi​ is the number written on the ithi^{th}ith card.


Output Format
For each test case, output the **minimum** number of moves required so that all the cards remaining on the table have the same number written on them.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the number of cards. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN, the numbers written on the cards.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer – the minimum number of moves required so that all remaining cards have the same number.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: frequencies are {1:2,2:2,3:1}, maxFreq=2, moves=5-2=3.\nTest case 2: all four cards are equal, maxFreq=4, moves=0.\nTest case 3: all numbers are distinct, maxFreq=1, moves=6-1=5.
First test: all cards already equal, no removal needed. Second test: all values distinct, keep any one card, remove the other four.
Frequency of 3 is 3, which is maximal. Moves = 7 - 3 = 4.

solution.approach:
The only operation allowed is removing a card. To keep the maximum possible cards on the table with the same value we should keep all cards of the value that appears most frequently. Let maxFreq be the highest frequency of any number in the array. Then we need to remove all other cards, i.e., N - maxFreq moves. The algorithm for each test case:
1. Read N and the array.
2. Count frequencies of each value (using an array if the range is small or a hash map otherwise).
3. Determine maxFreq.
4. Answer = N - maxFreq.
This is optimal because any solution must discard every card whose value is not the chosen final value, and keeping the value with the largest count minimizes deletions.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case – each element is processed once for counting.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(K) where K is the number of distinct values (at most N).
